source_text: [2] The passage 'Wundur ... búan' (3063-3066), M. took to be a question asking whether it was strange that a man should die when his appointed time had come.--B. sees a corruption, and makes emendations introducing the idea that a brave man should not die from sickness or from old age, but should find death in the performance of some deed of daring.--S. sees an indirect question introduced by 'hwár' and dependent upon 'wundur': A secret is it when the hero is to die, etc.--Why may the two clauses not be parallel, and the whole passage an Old English cry of 'How wonderful is death!'?
